report_name: npm Ecosystem Under Siege as Shai-Hulud Successors Weaponize CI/CD Pipelines

threat_actors.name: TeamPCP
threat_actors.description: A prolific threat actor group deploying next-generation wormable malware like Mini Shai-Hulud and Miasma to compromise developer accounts and abuse CI/CD pipelines in the npm ecosystem. The group executed a credential-free attack against TanStack's CI pipeline and open-sourced their malware.
threat_actors.goals: Credential harvesting from developer environments and further propagation of malware through the software supply chain.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Initial access was achieved using compromised developer credentials, such as a Red Hat employee's GitHub account, to inject malicious payloads into software packages.

attack_patterns.name: Access Token Manipulation
attack_patterns.description: Attackers abused CI/CD workflows in GitHub Actions to request and use OpenID Connect (OIDC) tokens to gain permissions to publish malicious packages to the npm registry.

attack_patterns.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
attack_patterns.description: The Miasma malware payload was injected as a heavily obfuscated index.js file, increasing its size by 25 times to evade detection.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: Attackers compromised CI/CD pipelines to build and publish trojanized npm packages, which appeared as legitimate software from publishers like Red Hat and TanStack.

attack_patterns.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript
attack_patterns.description: Malicious JavaScript within compromised npm packages executed upon installation via 'npm install', initiating the payload's credential theft and propagation routines.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Password Stores
attack_patterns.description: The malware scours the victim developer's machine and environment variables for sensitive credentials, including API keys for AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, and tokens for npm and GitHub.

malware.name: Shai-Hulud
malware.description: A worm that emerged in the npm ecosystem in September 2025, setting a precedent for systematic, high-impact supply chain attacks.

malware.name: Mini Shai-Hulud
malware.description: A next-generation, self-propagating worm deployed by TeamPCP. It is used to steal credentials and backdoor developer projects for further propagation. Its source code was publicly released by TeamPCP.

malware.name: Miasma
malware.description: A wormable malware payload derived from Mini Shai-Hulud. It was injected into Red Hat cloud services packages, abusing GitHub Actions and OIDC tokens to publish malicious versions. It performs credential theft and propagation.

campaigns.name: Red Hat Cloud Services Compromise
campaigns.description: A supply chain attack on June 1, 2026, where an attacker used a compromised Red Hat employee's GitHub account to inject the Miasma payload into 32 packages under the @redhat-cloud-services npm namespace.

campaigns.name: TanStack CI/CD Pipeline Attack
campaigns.description: A credential-free attack on May 11, 2026, attributed to TeamPCP, which compromised the CI pipeline for TanStack by chaining GitHub Actions weaknesses, leading to the publication of 84 malicious artifacts.

campaigns.name: Mini Shai-Hulud Waves
campaigns.description: A series of campaigns throughout April and May 2026, attributed to TeamPCP, that deployed variants of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm to steal credentials and propagate by backdooring developer projects.

identities.name: Red Hat
identities.description: A major software company whose cloud services packages on npm were compromised by the Miasma malware.

identities.name: TanStack
identities.description: A popular developer tooling provider whose CI/CD pipeline was compromised by TeamPCP, leading to the malicious publication of 84 artifacts.

identities.name: Bitwarden
identities.description: A legitimate software provider whose '@bitwarden/cli' package was impersonated by attackers to distribute credential-stealing malware.

identities.name: npm
identities.description: The public package registry targeted by the supply chain attacks. Malicious packages were published to npm to achieve widespread distribution.

identities.name: GitHub
identities.description: The code hosting and CI/CD platform whose features, such as GitHub Actions and OIDC tokens, were abused by attackers to compromise build pipelines and publish malicious packages.

infrastructure.name: Compromised CI/CD Pipeline
infrastructure.description: Attacker-controlled or compromised CI/CD infrastructure, such as GitHub Actions workflows, used to inject malicious code, build, and publish trojanized software packages.

indicators.name: Malicious Impersonation of @bitwarden/cli Package
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Malicious npm Package Impersonating Bitwarden CLI
id: 2b9a1c8d-f3e4-4a1b-9c8d-e2f3a4b5c6d7
status: experimental
description: Detects the presence of a file or directory named '@bitwarden/cli', which was observed being impersonated in npm supply chain attacks.
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-03
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|contains: '\@bitwarden\cli'
  condition: selection

indicators.name: Anomalous npm Install Child Process
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Child Process Spawned by npm Install
id: 8c7b6a5d-4e3f-4d2c-8b9a-0c1d2e3f4a5b
status: experimental
description: Detects 'npm.exe' or 'npm' spawning unexpected child processes like curl, bash, or powershell, which could indicate malicious pre/post-install scripts.
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-03
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
tags:
  - attack.t1059.007
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_parent:
    ParentImage|endswith: 
      - '\npm.cmd'
      - '\npm.exe'
  selection_child:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\curl.exe'
      - '\bash.exe'
      - '\powershell.exe'
      - '\wget.exe'
  condition: selection_parent and selection_child

indicators.name: Suspicious Access to npm Configuration File
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious Access to npm Configuration File
id: 1a2b3c4d-5e6f-4a9b-8c7d-6e5f4a3b2c1d
status: experimental
description: Detects processes accessing the .npmrc file, which contains authentication tokens. This can indicate credential theft by malicious packages.
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-03
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
tags:
  - attack.t1555
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith: '\.npmrc'
  filter_legit:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\npm.cmd'
      - '\npm.exe'
      - '\node.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_legit

relationships.source_name: Suspicious Access to npm Configuration File
